passages.explanation: Six days before Passover, Jesus returns to Bethany where Lazarus lives — the man he raised from the dead. They hold a dinner for him. Martha serves, Lazarus sits at the table. Mary takes a pound of expensive pure nard perfume, anoints Jesus' feet, and wipes them with her hair. The fragrance fills the entire house. The act is extravagant, intimate, and public.

Judas Iscariot objects — the perfume was worth about 300 denarii, roughly a year's wages. He argues it should have been sold and the money given to the poor. John undercuts Judas' concern immediately: he didn't care about the poor. He was the group's treasurer and had been stealing from the money box.

Jesus defends Mary: leave her alone. She kept this for the day of his burial. He says the poor will always be present, but he won't. Jesus is acknowledging that his death is approaching, and he interprets Mary's act as preparation for it — whether she intended it that way or not.

A large crowd comes to Bethany, not just to see Jesus but to see Lazarus — a living witness to the most dramatic sign Jesus has performed. The chief priests decide to kill Lazarus too, because his existence is driving people to believe in Jesus. The miracle they can't explain, they plan to eliminate.

The next day, a large crowd coming for the festival hears Jesus is approaching Jerusalem. They take palm branches and go out to meet him, shouting 'Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord, the King of Israel!' Jesus finds a young donkey and rides it, fulfilling a prophecy from Zechariah about Israel's king coming humbly on a donkey. John notes the disciples didn't understand the significance of this at the time — only after Jesus was glorified did they connect it to Scripture. The crowd that witnessed the raising of Lazarus keeps spreading the word, which is why so many people come out. The Pharisees are frustrated: the whole world is going after him.

Some Greeks who came to Jerusalem for the festival approach Philip and ask to see Jesus. Philip tells Andrew, and together they tell Jesus. The detail matters: non-Jews are now seeking Jesus out, which signals his mission is expanding beyond Israel.

Jesus responds by saying the time has come for the Son of Man to be glorified. He uses a farming metaphor: a grain of wheat that falls into the ground and dies produces much fruit, but if it doesn't die it remains alone. Anyone who loves their life in this world will lose it; anyone who lets go of it will keep it for eternal life. Whoever serves him must follow him — and the Father will honour anyone who serves the Son.

Jesus admits his soul is troubled. He considers asking the Father to save him from what's coming, but says this is exactly why he came. He asks the Father to glorify his name. A voice comes from the sky: 'I have both glorified it and will glorify it again.' The crowd hears it — some think it was thunder, others say an angel spoke. Jesus says the voice was for their benefit, not his.

Jesus says now is the judgment of this world and the ruler of this world — the devil — will be driven out. When he is 'lifted up from the earth,' he will draw all people to himself. John explains that 'lifted up' refers to the manner of his death — crucifixion. The crowd is confused: their Scripture says the Messiah remains forever. How can the Son of Man be 'lifted up'? Who is this Son of Man?

Jesus tells them the light is with them for a little longer — they should walk while they have it before darkness overtakes them. He urges them to believe in the light so they can become children of light. Then he withdraws and hides. John reflects on the widespread unbelief despite all the signs Jesus performed, citing Isaiah: 'Who has believed our report?' and explaining that Isaiah also said God blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts. John adds that Isaiah said this because he saw Jesus' glory and spoke about him. Still, many rulers did believe — but they wouldn't confess it publicly because the Pharisees would expel them from the synagogue. They valued human approval more than God's.

Jesus makes a final public declaration. Believing in him means believing in the one who sent him. Seeing him means seeing the Father. He came as light into the world so that believers wouldn't remain in darkness. He doesn't judge those who hear his words and reject them — his purpose was to save, not to judge. But the words he has spoken will themselves serve as the standard of judgment on the last day. Everything he has said came from the Father's command, and that command is eternal life.
